utilityName: San Jose Water Cross-Connection Control and Backflow Program
dueBasis: San Jose Water says testing and recordkeeping are required on all backflow preventers at least once a year, and customers contacted by the Cross-Connection and Backflow Program are expected to act when a hazard survey identifies the need for protection.
coveredPropertyTypes: Domestic services where hazard surveys require protection
Pools, boilers, decorative fountains, and other cross-connection hazards
Commercial and residential customers identified by the program
coveredDeviceTypes: Lead-free double check assemblies where allowed
penalties: San Jose Water evaluates extension requests case by case, but customers identified by hazard survey still need to install protection and keep annual testing current under California cross-connection rules.
sourceExcerpt: San Jose Water is a strong California utility because it publishes hazard-assessment logic, annual testing requirements, fire-service coverage, and a specialized backflow department instead of a generic FAQ-only page.
verdictSummary: San Jose Water is a strong California utility because it combines annual testing, hazard-based install rules, fire-service coverage, and a dedicated backflow department.
whoIsAffected: Commercial and residential properties, fire services, irrigation systems, and other San Jose Water customers whose properties create actual or potential cross-connection hazards.
residentialNotes: San Jose Water is unusually usable for residential pages because it publicly says it offers testing support for both commercial and residential customers.
Single-family demand usually shows up through irrigation, pools, or another identified hazard.
commercialNotes: Commercial intent is strong because the utility runs hazard surveys and keeps a list of locations with the greatest need for backflow devices.
Fire services and some domestic services are explicitly called out.

annualTesting.summary: San Jose Water says testing and recordkeeping are required on all backflow preventers at least once a year, and its backflow department administers the cross-connection control program.
highlights: A dedicated team of licensed Cross Connection Specialists runs the program.
Customers contacted after a survey are expected to respond and bring the property into compliance.
workflowSteps: If San Jose Water contacts the property after a survey or review, confirm which device is required.
Keep the annual test and recordkeeping current.
Contact the backflow department if the assembly type or timing is unclear.

irrigation.summary: San Jose Water repeatedly names irrigation systems as common cross-connections that can pull residential and commercial properties into the program.
highlights: Irrigation systems are directly named as cross-connection hazards.
Hazard surveys drive whether protection is required on the service.
Annual testing and recordkeeping still apply once the assembly is required.
workflowSteps: Confirm the irrigation setup has been flagged by San Jose Water's hazard review.
Install the approved assembly if required.
Keep the irrigation backflow device on its annual testing cycle.

fireLine.summary: San Jose Water is worth a fire-line subpage because it explicitly says backflow prevention devices are required on fire services.
highlights: Fire services are named directly in the public FAQs.
The utility prefers lead-free reduced pressure assemblies in most conditions.
Annual testing and recordkeeping apply after the device is installed.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the protected service is a fire line or another high-hazard connection.
Use the assembly type San Jose Water accepts for that service.
Keep the annual testing record current under the backflow department's program.
Review whether San Jose Water has identified the property as a hazard site.
Install or maintain the required assembly type.
Keep annual testing and recordkeeping current.
Work with the backflow department if extension or assembly-selection questions come up.
failureHighlights: San Jose Water uses hazard surveys instead of vague boilerplate.
Fire services and some domestic services are directly included.
Annual testing and recordkeeping are explicit public requirements.

costBand.testingRange: Testing is shaped by the utility's annual-testing requirement and whatever assembly type the hazard survey requires.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest pricing depends on whether the device is serving fire, irrigation, or another high-hazard connection.
costBand.pricingNotes: The page's value is in hazard-based compliance clarity, not a posted retail rate.
